formulas.name: Lift Equation
formulas.explanation: This is the core lift model: lift grows with air density, wing area, lift coefficient, and especially with the square of airspeed.
formulas.useCase: Use this to explain why small speed increases can produce large lift changes, and why high density altitude hurts performance.

variations.name: Solve for Lift Coefficient
variations.explanation: Useful when lift is known and you need the required CL for the current speed/configuration.

variations.name: Solve for Required Airspeed
variations.explanation: Shows the speed required to produce a target lift at a given density and wing setup.

variations.name: Ratio Form
variations.explanation: Compares two conditions directly without solving absolute lift first.

simplifications.name: Speed-Only View
simplifications.explanation: If everything else is fixed, lift follows the square of speed.

simplifications.name: Double-Speed Rule
simplifications.explanation: Great quick check for oral questions on speed/lift sensitivity.

formulas.name: Basic Stall Speed
formulas.explanation: Stall speed rises with aircraft weight and falls with denser air, larger wing area, or higher maximum lift coefficient.
formulas.useCase: Use this relationship when discussing why heavier aircraft, high altitude, and icing (lower CLmax) increase stall speed.

variations.name: Solve for Weight
variations.explanation: Rearranged to estimate maximum supported weight at a chosen stall speed.

variations.name: Weight Ratio Form
variations.explanation: Valid when configuration, density, and CLmax are unchanged.

variations.name: Density Sensitivity
variations.explanation: Lower air density raises stall speed for the same aircraft and configuration.

simplifications.name: 10 Percent Weight Rule
simplifications.explanation: A quick way to estimate stall-speed increase from extra weight.

simplifications.name: 21 Percent Weight Rule
simplifications.explanation: Handy reverse estimate because 1.1 squared is about 1.21.

formulas.name: Stall Speed vs Load Factor
formulas.explanation: Stall speed increases with the square root of load factor. Pulling G in turns increases required lift and therefore raises stall speed.
formulas.useCase: A common oral/written application: at 60 degrees bank (about 2g), stall speed increases by about 41 percent.

variations.name: Direct 1g Form
variations.explanation: Most common PPL memory form when comparing to published clean/configured stall speed.

variations.name: Solve for Load Factor
variations.explanation: Useful to estimate g-loading from observed/required stall speed margin.

variations.name: Ratio Form
variations.explanation: Works for any two operating points, not only from 1g baseline.

simplifications.name: Common Multipliers
simplifications.explanation: Fast multipliers for mental checks in steep turns and maneuvering.

simplifications.name: Equivalent Statement
simplifications.explanation: Correct when G is the load factor n in g units.

formulas.name: Load Factor in a Level Coordinated Turn
formulas.explanation: As bank angle increases, cosine(theta) decreases, so load factor rises quickly. This ties bank angle directly to structural and stall margins.
formulas.useCase: Quick checkpoints: 45 degrees bank is about 1.41g, and 60 degrees bank is 2g.

variations.name: Solve for Cosine
variations.explanation: Intermediate step for converting known load factor into bank angle.

variations.name: Solve for Bank Angle
variations.explanation: Use to estimate bank from observed/desired load factor.

variations.name: Combined Stall-Speed Form
variations.explanation: Combines load-factor and stall-speed relations in one equation.

simplifications.name: Bank to G Quick Values
simplifications.explanation: Most-used checkpoints for maneuvering speed and stall margin discussion.

simplifications.name: Steep-Bank Warning
simplifications.explanation: Shows how quickly g-load rises beyond 60 degrees bank.

formulas.name: Moment
formulas.explanation: Moment is turning effect around a datum. Every weight station creates a moment based on its arm.
formulas.useCase: Compute each station's moment before summing total moments and total weights.

variations.name: Solve for Weight
variations.explanation: Useful if moment limit and arm are known and you need allowable weight.

variations.name: Solve for Arm
variations.explanation: Used when back-solving station location from tabulated weight and moment.

simplifications.name: Linear Scaling
simplifications.explanation: If arm does not change, moment scales directly with weight.

simplifications.name: Sign Convention
simplifications.explanation: Always follow the aircraft's datum/sign convention to avoid CG mistakes.

formulas.name: Center of Gravity
formulas.explanation: CG location is total moment divided by total weight. It gives the aircraft's balance point relative to datum.
formulas.useCase: After adding all stations, compare calculated CG with POH forward and aft limits.

variations.name: Solve for Total Moment
variations.explanation: Use when target CG and total weight are known.

variations.name: Solve for Total Weight
variations.explanation: Useful for reverse checks around loading limits.

simplifications.name: Small Change Approximation
simplifications.explanation: Quick estimate of CG shift after a small moment change.

simplifications.name: Unit Consistency
simplifications.explanation: Moment and weight units must match the POH table system.

formulas.name: CG Shift from Moved/Burned Weight
formulas.explanation: When weight moves or fuel burns, total moment changes by moved weight times distance moved. Divide by current total weight for new arm.
formulas.useCase: Use this for quick what-if checks, such as passenger seat changes or fuel burn over flight time.

variations.name: Moment Change First
variations.explanation: Compute delta moment first, then add/subtract from old total moment.

variations.name: CG Shift Form
variations.explanation: Direct CG movement expression often taught for quick load moves.

variations.name: Old to New Arm
variations.explanation: Alternative to recomputing full total moments each time.

simplifications.name: Direction Rule
simplifications.explanation: Good sanity check before finalizing numbers.

simplifications.name: Fuel Burn Note
simplifications.latex: \text{Fuel burn acts like removing weight at fuel arm}
simplifications.explanation: Apply the same equations with the fuel-tank arm and burned fuel weight.

formulas.name: Pressure Altitude
formulas.explanation: Pressure altitude converts local pressure setting into a standard reference altitude for performance calculations.
formulas.useCase: First step for density altitude and many performance chart lookups.

variations.name: Pressure Difference Term
variations.explanation: Altitude correction from local setting to standard pressure.

variations.name: Solve for Altimeter Setting
variations.explanation: Reverse form for checking setup/worksheet values.

simplifications.name: Per 0.01 inHg Rule
simplifications.explanation: Fast conversion for rough pressure-altitude corrections.

simplifications.name: Direction Check
simplifications.explanation: Lower pressure means higher pressure altitude.

formulas.name: ISA Temperature at Altitude
formulas.explanation: Standard atmosphere assumes about 2 degrees C cooling per 1000 ft from sea-level ISA of 15 degrees C.
formulas.useCase: Use this as the reference temperature when computing density altitude correction.

variations.name: Using Thousands of Feet
variations.explanation: Equivalent shorthand when altitude is in thousands of feet.

variations.name: Solve for Altitude from ISA Temp
variations.explanation: Useful in reverse problems where standard temp is given.

simplifications.name: Common Checkpoints
simplifications.explanation: Quick memory values for weather/performance briefing.

simplifications.name: Lapse Rate Approximation
simplifications.explanation: Use this gradient for fast ISA deltas in PPL-level planning.

formulas.name: Density Altitude (Rule of Thumb)
formulas.explanation: Density altitude rises when outside air temperature is above ISA and falls when cooler than ISA.
formulas.useCase: High DA means lower climb performance, longer takeoff roll, and poorer prop/wing efficiency.

variations.name: DA Offset Form
variations.explanation: Shows DA correction directly as a temperature-delta term.

variations.name: Solve for OAT
variations.explanation: Reverse form when DA and PA are known.

simplifications.name: Per Degree Rule
simplifications.explanation: Most-used head math for DA impact.

simplifications.name: 10 Degree Rule
simplifications.explanation: Good quick check for hot-day performance degradation.

formulas.name: True Airspeed Approximation
formulas.explanation: A quick correction: TAS is roughly 2 percent higher than CAS for each 1000 ft of altitude.
formulas.useCase: Good for fast mental estimates, but use POH/E6B/CX-2 for precise exam or performance questions.

variations.name: Additive Form
variations.explanation: Same formula written as base speed plus altitude correction.

variations.name: Solve for CAS Approximation
variations.explanation: Reverse mental estimate if TAS is known.

simplifications.name: 3000 ft Rule
simplifications.explanation: About 6 percent increase around 3000 ft.

simplifications.name: 6000 ft Rule
simplifications.explanation: About 12 percent increase around 6000 ft.

formulas.name: Groundspeed / Time / Distance
formulas.explanation: This is the base triangle relation for all navigation timing problems. Rearranging gives time or distance.
formulas.useCase: Use as Time = Distance/GS and Distance = GS x Time for enroute calculations and ETA planning.

variations.name: Solve for Time
variations.explanation: Primary form for ETA calculation.

variations.name: Solve for Distance
variations.explanation: Use to estimate progress or leg distance covered.

variations.name: Minutes Form
variations.explanation: Convenient when stopwatch time is in minutes.

simplifications.name: NM per Minute
simplifications.explanation: Convert knots to per-minute pace for checkpoint planning.

simplifications.name: Quick Example
simplifications.explanation: Useful benchmark for rough timing.

formulas.name: 60-to-1 Time Rule
formulas.explanation: Converts distance and speed directly into minutes and is one of the most-used mental math tools in PPL nav questions.
formulas.useCase: Very useful for checkpoint ETAs and quick replans after unexpected winds.

variations.name: Solve for Distance
variations.explanation: Useful when you have elapsed minutes and groundspeed.

variations.name: Solve for Groundspeed
variations.explanation: Good for back-solving actual GS from observed progress.

simplifications.name: 120 kt Shortcut
simplifications.explanation: Easy benchmark for quick enroute calculations.

simplifications.name: 90 kt Shortcut
simplifications.explanation: Common GA cruise benchmark.

formulas.name: Off-Course Distance (60-to-1)
formulas.explanation: Equivalent to the rule that 1 degree error causes about 1 NM offset after 60 NM flown.
formulas.useCase: Estimate how far off track you are, then determine correction/closing angles.

variations.name: Solve for Track Error
variations.explanation: Useful when offset is observed and you need heading correction logic.

variations.name: Closing Angle
variations.explanation: Classic second step after computing current off-track distance.

simplifications.name: Core Memory Rule
simplifications.explanation: Baseline mental anchor for all 60-to-1 drift questions.

simplifications.name: Half-Distance Rule
simplifications.explanation: Quick interpolation without full formula.

formulas.name: Endurance
formulas.explanation: Endurance is how long you can keep flying at a given fuel consumption rate.
formulas.useCase: Use this for reserve checks and to compare planned leg time against safe usable endurance.

variations.name: Solve for Usable Fuel
variations.explanation: Useful when planning fuel required for a target block time.

variations.name: Solve for Burn Rate
variations.explanation: Back-calculate fuel flow from observed endurance.

variations.name: Range Link
variations.explanation: Connects endurance directly to distance capability.

simplifications.name: Reserve-Aware Fuel
simplifications.explanation: Always remove legal/safety reserve before endurance planning.

simplifications.name: Reserve Time Margin
simplifications.explanation: Quick check for whether the leg keeps required reserve intact.

formulas.name: 3 Degree Descent Rate Approximation
formulas.explanation: A standard 3 degree path needs roughly five times groundspeed as feet-per-minute descent.
formulas.useCase: Example: 90 kt groundspeed needs about 450 fpm descent for a stable 3 degree path.

variations.name: Solve for Groundspeed
variations.explanation: Reverse check if desired descent rate is constrained.

variations.name: More Exact 3 Deg Form
variations.explanation: 5.3 is closer to geometric 3 degree slope; 5 is easier mental math.

simplifications.name: Common Values
simplifications.explanation: Fast target settings for stable visual/instrument-style descents.

simplifications.name: Mental Range
simplifications.explanation: Use 5 for speed, 5.3 for tighter precision.

variables.meaning: rate of descent in feet per minute

formulas.name: Top of Descent Approximation
formulas.explanation: At a 3 degree descent, every 300 ft of altitude loss needs about 1 NM of distance.
formulas.useCase: Plan when to begin descent without rushing the approach setup.

variations.name: Solve for Altitude to Lose
variations.explanation: Useful when TOD distance is fixed and you need descent target.

variations.name: Thousands-of-Feet Form
variations.explanation: Very common pilot shorthand for quick setup.

simplifications.name: Easy Example
simplifications.explanation: Simple benchmark for arrival briefing.

simplifications.name: 6000 ft Example
simplifications.explanation: Another common practical reference point.

formulas.name: Turn Radius
formulas.explanation: Turn radius increases with the square of speed and decreases with steeper bank angle.
formulas.useCase: Explains why high-speed turns need a lot of space and why slow, steeper turns can tighten radius.

variations.name: Solve for Airspeed
variations.explanation: Find speed for a desired radius at a chosen bank angle.

variations.name: Solve for Bank Angle
variations.explanation: Estimate required bank to achieve a target radius at current speed.

simplifications.name: Speed-Squared Effect
simplifications.explanation: Shows why fast aircraft need much larger turn space.

simplifications.name: Bank Effect
simplifications.explanation: Steeper bank reduces radius, but increases load factor and stall speed.

formulas.name: Standard-Rate Turn Bank Approximation
formulas.explanation: Quick estimate for bank angle needed to achieve about 3 degrees per second turn rate.
formulas.useCase: Helpful in instrument-style timed turns and oral questions about standard-rate technique.

variations.name: Solve for TAS
variations.explanation: Reverse form for rough speed estimate from known standard-rate bank.

variations.name: Exact Turn-Rate Relation
variations.explanation: Use when a question asks beyond the rule-of-thumb approximation.

simplifications.name: Common Approximations
simplifications.explanation: Practical values to check whether your turn setup is reasonable.

simplifications.name: Rule Scope
simplifications.latex: \text{Valid as quick mental math, not a POH replacement}
simplifications.explanation: Use exact methods/E6B when exam or procedure precision is required.
